## Summary
Global Workspace Theory (GWT) proposes that consciousness arises when information is broadcast globally across the brain via a "workspace" of interconnected cortical areas, primarily involving prefrontal and parietal cortices. Unconscious processes compete for access to this workspace; those that win are consciously experienced.

## Evidence
Dehaene's Global Neuronal Workspace variant predicts an ignition pattern — nonlinear amplification when stimuli reach awareness — observed in EEG (P3b wave) and fMRI (frontoparietal activation). The theory correctly predicts attentional blink, subliminal processing, and binocular rivalry dynamics. The 2023 adversarial collaboration with IIT found evidence for ignition-like dynamics but questioned prefrontal necessity.

## Significance
GWT is the most empirically tractable theory of consciousness, with clear predictions about neural mechanisms. It naturally explains why we can report conscious experiences (broadcasting makes information available to speech, memory, and planning systems) but has been criticized for conflating access consciousness with phenomenal consciousness.
